What Is Humanities Research Data?
=================================

*   [Digital Scholarship Services](https://dss.lafayette.edu/)
*   [What DSS Can Do For You](https://dss.lafayette.edu/resources/)
*   [Humanities Research Data](https://dss.lafayette.edu/resources-2/)
*   [What Is Humanities Research Da…](https://dss.lafayette.edu/what-is-humanities-research-data/)

Subnavigation

*   [What DSS Can Do For You](https://dss.lafayette.edu/resources/)
    *   [Omeka S and Scalar Scope of Service](https://dss.lafayette.edu/omeka-s-and-scalar-scope-of-service/)
    *   [DSS Collaborative Support Program](https://dss.lafayette.edu/dss-collaborative-support-program/)
    *   [Digitization Services for Research & Teaching](https://dss.lafayette.edu/digitization-services-for-research-teaching/)
    *   [Scope of Work Planning](https://dss.lafayette.edu/memorandum-of-understanding/)
    *   [Digital Pedagogy](https://dss.lafayette.edu/digital-pedagogy/)
    *   [Humanities Research Data](https://dss.lafayette.edu/resources-2/)
        *   [What Is Humanities Research Data?](https://dss.lafayette.edu/what-is-humanities-research-data/)
        *   [Humanities Research Data Management Planning](https://dss.lafayette.edu/humanities-research-data-management-planning/)

"Digital humanities," or DH, is the use of digital technologies to pursue research questions in the humanities. Research data is the raw material we need to examine these questions.

When you think of the “digital humanities,” you might picture visualizations such as word clouds, multimedia web-publishing projects, or interactive historic maps. All digital humanities projects are built using data, so early considerations for DH projects of any scale are:

*   What data to use
*   Which formats data need to be in, depending on the platforms and tools involved
*   Where to collect and store data during project building

Examples of humanities research data include:

*   text files extracted from a corpus of texts by Optical Character Recognition software
*   image files of archival items or artworks
*   geospatial data including raster and vector files
*   oral history sound files and transcripts
*   archival metadata

**DIGITAL ARCHIVES AS DATA**

Digital collections are rich sources of humanities research data. Increasingly, libraries, museums, and other cultural heritage institutions offer digital collections among their holdings. See the[Lafayette Special Collections & College Archives digital collections](https://archives.lafayette.edu/digital-collections/), for instance.

Further resources for digital archival research data:

_Open access digital collections, such as:_

*   [Library of Congress Digital Collections](https://www.loc.gov/collections/)
*   [Smithsonian Libraries Digital Collections](http://library.si.edu/collections/all)
*   [New York Public Library Digital Collections](https://digitalcollections.nypl.org/)
*   [British Library Digital Collections](https://www.bl.uk/collection-guides)

_Open-access multi-institutional collaboratives:_ the[Digital Public Library of America](https://dp.la/)and the[HathiTrust Digital Library and Research Center](https://www.hathitrust.org/htrc)are hubs where multiple libraries share resources for scholarly use.

_Proprietary digital archival databases_ such as [Adam Matthew primary source collections](https://www.amdigital.co.uk/). You may be able to request your library to fund access to proprietary databases.

**Archival metadata** is another rich source of humanities research data for digital scholarship. For an intriguing model, see DH scholar Anne Donlon’s project “[From Encoded Archival Description to Network Graphs](http://annedonlon.org/from-encoded-archival-description-to-network-graphs/).” The [British Library Labs](http://labs.bl.uk/Digital+Collections)provide guides and datasets for digital scholarship using archival metadata.

Resources:

*   “[Library Collections as Humanities Data](https://networks.h-net.org/node/14775/discussions/51378/%E2%80%9Clibrary-collections-humanities-data-facet-effect%E2%80%9D)” by Thomas Padilla and Devin Higgins
*   “[Always Already Computational: Library Collections as Data](https://collectionsasdata.github.io/aac_positionstatements.pdf)”
*   Proceedings of the 2016 [“Collections as Data” conference](http://digitalpreservation.gov/meetings/dcs16.html) at the Library of Congress

# Digital Humanities: DH Books & Journals

## Finding Books & Journals on DH

This page holds a selection of journals and strategies to find books on DH. For additional journals or articles, check the [library catalog](https://catalog.library.fordham.edu/uhtbin/cgisirsi/x/SIRSI/0/57/60/495/X?user_id=WEBSERVER) for a specific journal or search by keyword within [subject-specific databases](https://fordham.libguides.com/ElectronicResources/Home).

![Reminder](https://libapps.s3.amazonaws.com/accounts/107186/images/1298504605-300px.png) If you need assistance, use the [Ask a Librarian](https://www.fordham.edu/info/27156/libraries/8597/ask_a_librarian) service or contact on the the [library liaisons for DH](https://fordham.libguides.com/DigitalHumanities).

![Reminder](https://libapps.s3.amazonaws.com/accounts/107186/images/1298504605-300px.png)

Below is a selection of journals that cover DH, digital scholarship, digital pedagogy, etc.

To search across multiple journals on a topic, [try a subject-specific database](https://fordham.libguides.com/ElectronicResources/Home).

--- SOURCE 5: Data sources for analyzing open access offers from publishers ---
URL: https://sparcopen.org/our-work/negotiation-resources/data-analysis/data-sources-for-analyzing-open-access-offers-from-publishers/

SUMMARY:
An analyst would use Web of Science to gather data about a specific article or set of articles—a vast amount of information about each article is available and is indexed for search, making WoS a valuable tool for understanding, for example, what (or how much) has been published in a given journal, from a given publisher, or by authors with a given affiliation. OA-related analyses, in particular, include using the embedded Unpaywall data to measure the portion of a given set of articles that is openly available through various pathways (full OA journals, hybrid OA, repositories, etc.) to assess how much content would be available without a subscription. Knowledge base lists are useful for identifying open access journals that are included in publisher package lists or COUNTER reports.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Open access academic journals like the Journal of Open Humanities Data provide peer-reviewed research, ensuring broad accessibility to critical humanities data for scholars and practitioners.
Institutional repositories, such as those found in libraries and universities, host a wealth of digital archives, offering access to a diverse range of humanities research materials.
Conducting interviews with researchers offers qualitative insights, enriching humanities research by capturing personal experiences and perspectives on open access practices and challenges.
